blocks.text: The **Model Context Protocol** is the open standard that connects AI applications to tools and data. Three roles: the **Host** (the chat app — claude.ai, Claude Desktop, an IDE), the **Client** (the protocol endpoint the host embeds, one per server), and the **Server** (exposes tools, resources, and prompts). Transport is **JSON-RPC 2.0** over stdio or HTTP.

items.label: initialize
items.text: Client and server exchange capabilities and protocol versions

items.label: tools/list
items.text: The server advertises its tools — name, description, JSON-Schema input contract

items.label: model decides
items.text: Tool descriptions enter the model's context; the model chooses when a tool call serves the conversation

items.label: tools/call
items.text: The host executes the call against the server and returns the result into context

blocks.text: **Where the model sits:** between the conversation and the tool call. The server never sees the chat; the host mediates everything — consent prompts, rate limits, result routing. A tool's JSON-Schema is a *contract*, not a suggestion: hosts validate arguments before anything executes.

cards.back: The AI application the user talks to. Owns consent, execution, and what enters the model's context.
A process exposing tools/resources/prompts over JSON-RPC. Never sees the conversation — only the calls the host forwards.
A callable capability with a name, description, and JSON-Schema input contract. The description is what the model reasons over.
Addressable content (files, data, UI templates) a server exposes by URI for hosts to read.
The wire framing: request {jsonrpc, id, method, params} → response {result | error}. Notifications omit the id.

blocks.question: Which party decides whether a tool call actually executes?
options: The server — it owns the tool
The model — it emitted the call
The host — it mediates consent and execution
blocks.explanation: The model proposes; the host disposes. Consent, validation, execution, and what flows back into context are all host responsibilities — that mediation is MCP's security model.

points: MCP = host ↔ client ↔ server over JSON-RPC 2.0
Tools are typed contracts; descriptions are what the model reasons over
The server never sees the chat — the host mediates everything
Consent and validation live at the host, by design
